coredata.dc:title: Enhanced UV-sensing properties by utilizing solution-processed GQD in GQDs/Porous Si heterojunction Near-UV photodetector
coredata.dc:creator: Kalantari, Fatemeh
Amirmazlaghani, Mina
Olyaee, Saeed
coredata.dc:description: This article evaluates the performance and photo-response of a GQDs (Graphene Quantum Dots)/Porous Si heterostructure near-UV sensor and compares it with the bare Porous Si device, indicating its effect on improving the sensor characteristics. The steps taken to fabricate each nanostructure and photosensor are explained. The morphology of each nanostructure is studied. The solution of GQDs is also examined by UV–Vis and FTIR spectroscopy. The photo-response of devices with and without GQD layers to near-ultraviolet light irradiation at 395 nm is investigated. With the inclusion of GQDs in the structure, the photocurrent and responsivity of the device are significantly improved. GQDs/Porous Si heterostructure UV photosensor demonstrates a responsivity of 19.41 A/W under a light power density of 21 μW/cm2 at room temperature, which is considerably increased compared to the absence of GQDs in the structure (0.49 A/W). The detectivity of 7.5 × 1012 Jones is reached, and the EQE (external quantum efficiency) stand at 6093%. The findings show the influential role of GQDs in the structure owing to their superior optical characteristics. The detection mechanism is also explained based on the band structure of the device.

Quantum dot
UV sensor
Heterostructure
Porous Si

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of GQDs/Porous Si heterojunction photodetector

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a. The process of making graphene quantum dots by pyrolysis approach from citric acid as raw material. b. schematic diagram of GQDs/Porous Si heterojunction under 395 nm near UV light illumination.

a. TEM image of as-prepared GQDs by pyrolysis of citric acid. b. diameter distribution histogram of GQDs, average diameter is about 9.4 nm c. UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs solution, it has two peaks in UV region. The inset shows the GQDs solution under visible light (pale yellow) and under UV illumination (blue emission). d. Tauc plot that shows optical band gap of GQDs is 2.94eV. The π→
π *
electron transition as a result of sp2 electron excitation in carbon atoms and the excitation which resulted in n→
π *
transition are the reason for other probable energy gaps in the Tauc plot e. FTIR spectrum of GQDs that exhibit the present various functional groups in GQDs solution. f. Photoluminescent spectrum of the GQDs synthesized with the same approach as we proposed in this paper, the spectrum is shown under different excitation wavelength [38].

FESEM images of the body structure of photodetector a. Ag nanoparticles coated silicon wafer before etching process with an average diameter of 68 nm. It shows a complete and uniform surface coverage of the wafer. b magnified top view shows clustered Porous Si arrays. c. top view image of the wafer after the etching process. Porous Si are made on the whole Ag-coated wafer, and the diameter is in the 75–85 nm range. d. cross-section view of bare Porous Si that have a columnar structure with a length of 29.2 μm. e. The GQDs-coated Porous Si SEM image that is zoomed in f.

Current-voltage curves related to the Porous Si and GQDs/Porous Si structures in the dark and under ultraviolet light illumination with power density of 1400 μW/cm2 in (a) linear and (b) semi-logarithmic scales. As can be seen, the current is voltage dependent and increases significantly with increasing voltage under radiation. (c) Diagram of light current to dark current ratio. (d) Relationship between sensitivity of structures to illumination power density, (e) Semi-logarithmic diagram of light current of two structures based on different illumination power densities, which shows the increasing relationship of current with power.

Diagram of the photodetector parameters calculated for the bare Porous Si structure (a, c, e) and the GQDs/Porous Si heterostructure (b, d, f) at different applied bias voltages in terms of illumination power density.

Schematic of the band diagram of a) Porous Si and b) GQDs/Porous Si heterostructure and their up and bottom contact under illumination. In the bare Si samples, Si forms a Schottky junction with Ag and an ohmic contact with Al. In GQDs/porous Si heterostructure, GQDs form a Schottky-like junction with Si, while Al and Ag form ohmic contacts to Si and GQDs, respectively.
a) The I–V curve of GQDs/porous Si junction under UV, red and white radiations. b) the reversed bias mode is zoomed. The device shows the better response under UV radiations.

a) Photocurrent of photodetector under 850 nm and 940 nm irradiation with two different power densities. As can be seen, by increasing the light power density, the photocurrent is increased at all bias points. b) responsivity, c) detectivity and d) external quantum efficiency of GQDs/porous Si Schottky heterojunction to two IR radiations with 850 nm and 940 nm wavelengths. The responsivity, detectivity and external efficiency are shown as a function of the reversed bias voltage and also shown for two different powers of the sources. As can be seen by increasing the reversed bias voltage, the responsivity, detectivity and efficiency are increased. It is due to the well-known image force lowering effect that decrease the potential barrier in front of the carriers in reversed bias mode. Additionally, it is observed that the responsivity, detectivity and external efficiency are dependent to the power of the source which is probably due to the saturation of the photo-generated current in the device.
Resistance values for structures of Porous Si and GQDs/Porous Si in the dark and under illumination.
A brief comparison between photodetector parameters in some previous works and the present work.

head.ce:title: Enhanced UV-sensing properties by utilizing solution-processed GQD in GQDs/Porous Si heterojunction Near-UV photodetector

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article evaluates the performance and photo-response of a GQDs (Graphene Quantum Dots)/Porous Si heterostructure near-UV sensor and compares it with the bare Porous Si device, indicating its effect on improving the sensor characteristics. The steps taken to fabricate each nanostructure and photosensor are explained. The morphology of each nanostructure is studied. The solution of GQDs is also examined by UV–Vis and FTIR spectroscopy. The photo-response of devices with and without GQD layers to near-ultraviolet light irradiation at 395 nm is investigated. With the inclusion of GQDs in the structure, the photocurrent and responsivity of the device are significantly improved. GQDs/Porous Si heterostructure UV photosensor demonstrates a responsivity of 19.41 A/W under a light power density of 21 μW/cm2 at room temperature, which is considerably increased compared to the absence of GQDs in the structure (0.49 A/W). The detectivity of 7.5 × 1012 Jones is reached, and the EQE (external quantum efficiency) stand at 6093%. The findings show the influential role of GQDs in the structure owing to their superior optical characteristics. The detection mechanism is also explained based on the band structure of the device.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Silicon, the second most abundant element on Earth, with properties such as high thermal conductivity and hardness, low density, and cost-effective manufacturing process, has been a critical and prominent semiconductor material in various research fields for decades. Its remarkable optical and electrical tunable properties have played a significant role in numerous applications, e.g., nano-electronics, optoelectronics, energy conversion, and sensing. Due to its unique characteristics, silicon is preferred in the structure of photodetectors, which are responsible for converting an optical signal into an electrical signal. It usually performs well in the visible and near-infrared (NIR) wavelength range [1–4]. However, despite the abovementioned, the response of silicon detectors in the ultraviolet region is not appropriate due to the indirect small energy gap (1.12 eV), low surface penetration of ultraviolet light in this material, and high light reflection from the silicon surface (about 38% [5], that leads to low absorption). It has limited use of silicon-based detectors in the ultraviolet region [3,6].

Ultraviolet photodetectors have received considerable research attention owing to their extensive applications in optical communication, space communication, remote control, medical diagnostics, image sensing, and national safety fields, e.g., flame or ozone monitoring [7–13]. The use of wide-gap semiconductor materials such as GaN, AlGaN, and ZnO in the structure of UV photodetectors has been investigated. However, due to the high cost of these materials, they are not economical for large-scale use. Moreover, their combination with silicon as a structural block in ultraviolet detectors does not have the desired sensitivity for detection because of these materials' lattice mismatch [8].

The strategy of using one-dimensional silicon nanostructures such as Porous Si and Si nanopillar has recently been proposed and studied to enhance the response of silicon photodetectors in the ultraviolet region. Thanks to their easy and inexpensive fabrication and adaptation to current silicon microelectronic processes [14], silicon nanostructures are a major element in the structures of electronic and optical components on a small scale (micro and nano) and chemical and biological sensors [15–17]. Porous silicon, a form of silicon that has a sponge-like structure due to its holes [18,19], has exceptional characteristics such as high resistance, chemical activity, visible photoluminescence at room temperature, large surface-to-volume ratio, direct and broad gap, and most importantly, a crystal structure similar to bulk silicon that solves the lattice mismatch problem, making it attractive to the research community [20–23]. These structures are environmentally friendly and have controllable optical and electrical properties. A high aspect ratio in structures such as Porous Si not only provides better light absorption [22,24] but also improves the diffusion length of carriers and shortens their travel path with structures; consequently, it traps and collects carriers more efficiently and reduces their recombination, ultimately improving the performance of the device [2–4,23]. On the other hand, due to the ability of this structure to drastically reduce reflection compared to bulk silicon [11], there is no need to use light-reflecting layers in applications such as solar cells, which reduces the structure thickness and size, as well as manufacturing costs [25]. The methods of fabricating silicon nanostructures are diverse, including electrochemical etching, VLS, stain etching, and RIE [26,27]. Among them, the top-down metal-assisted chemical etching (MACE) is significant due to its excellent features, such as a fast and straightforward process, reliability, cost-effectiveness, and compliance with CMOS techniques. The nanostructures obtained with this method have controllable size, density, and crystal orientation [4,15,23,28].

The use of heterostructures between Porous Si and a semiconductor material with a wide energy gap is a step forward in improving the performance of detectors based on these materials. Among semiconductor materials that have been extensively studied in recent years, zero-dimensional luminescence quantum dots drew considerable attention because of their high photosensitivity [29], spectral down-conversion properties [3,6], and the ability to combine with silicon structures. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are usually obtained by restricting graphene in all three dimensions. These semiconductors have outstanding electrical and optical properties and are utilized in optoelectronics, photovoltaics, biosensing, and energy storage components, mainly because of their edge effects and quantum confinement effect [30–32]. The excellent properties of these materials include non-toxicity [6,10,33], a wide and adjustable energy gap [9,10,30], large and size-dependent absorption and emission of light (due to carboxyl and surface hydroxyl functional groups [32]), and solid chemical stability [10,13]. The presence of graphene quantum dots as part of the structure reduces the recombination of photo-generated carriers, increases optical conductivity, and accelerates the transfer rate [9]. For example, owing to its excellent edge effect and high light absorption, the ZnO-based superstructure UV detector doped by GQDs (ZnO superstructure/GQDs) creates a freeway that effectively separates the charge carriers, thereby diminishing the recombination rate and improving the optical properties [32]. The use of GQDs as a sensitizing agent in the TiO2-based photoelectrochemical UV photodetector structure improved the photocurrent by more than 40% and, thus, the structure's responsivity. These enhancements have been reported due to improved light-harvesting efficiency and charge collection efficiency in the presence of GQDs [10]. Furthermore, the size and localized defect states of GQDs affect its spectral characteristics, and its spectral down-converting nature leads to a good response in GQDs/n-Si heterojunction detectors compared to bare Si [6].

In this paper, vertical nanostructures of silicon (Porous Si) were fabricated using the fast and easy MACE method, and their morphology was investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Solution-processed graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized from citric acid as a raw material via a simple, green, and low-cost bottom-up pyrolysis approach. Its structural morphology and optical characteristics were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–Vis, and FTIR spectroscopies. Drop-coating the GQDs on the porous Si forms the UV detector on a heterostructure between the Porous Si and the GQDs, with a thick layer of aluminum as the bottom contact and silver paste as the top contact. The optoelectrical characteristics of the GQDs/Porous Si heterostructure were investigated in the dark and under illumination, and the data were evaluated. A responsivity of 19.41 A/W under a light power density of 21 μW/cm2, detectivity of 7.5 × 1012 Jones, and an EQE of 6093% were determined at room temperature. The detection mechanism is also explained based on the band structure of the device.

Commercial n-type Si (100) wafer with low phosphorous doping, resistivity of 1–10 Ω cm, and thickness of 525 ± 25 μm were cut into 1 × 1 cm2 square pieces. The wafers were cleaned in acetone, ethanol, and propanol (in 15 min) using an ultrasonic bath to remove surface dust; were rinsed with deionized (DI) water between each step, and eventually dried under the N2 flow. To remove other organic and inorganic contamination, the wafers were soaked in Piranha solution (H2SO4 and H2O2 with 3:1 v/v) for 10 min at room temperature and washed with DI water again. As reported in different references, the porous nanostructure on the Si surface was fabricated using the metal-assisted chemical etching (MACE) method. Briefly, 0.02 mg of silver nitrate (AgNO3) and 4.8 M aqueous hydrogen fluoride (HF) solution were mixed to prepare the etchant solution for metallization before etching. The Ag nanoparticles were obtained on the silicon surface by immersing the clean Si wafer in an etchant solution for 40 s under ambient conditions, then rinsing with DI water to remove extra Ag+ ions and letting it dry at room temperature. The Ag-deposited wafer was then immersed in the etchant solution consisting of HF acid and fresh hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) at a volume ratio of 1:3 for 20 min at room temperature. Finally, the etched samples were washed with DI water and dried at room temperature.

The porous Si is a fluorescent material in 600–700 nm wavelength and the photoluminescence property of porous silicon is usually used as a sign of creating porosity in silicon during the production process. The change of Si color to slime green or slime orange is good evidence of pores creating in Si substrate, especially in the chemical process in comparison with the electrochemical process. In the electrochemical method, because the current of the cell is usually large and strongly promotes the oxidation, not much oxide remains on the surface and the pores’ depth is also high. But in the chemical method, as the pores are created with the help of oxidation and removal with acid, the depth is low, the oxide is high, and as a result, a visible spectrum is yield. It is worth to note that the size and photoluminescence properties of the porous Si can be controlled by some parameters such as the current density and electrolyte concentration which leads to different PL spectrums [34–36].

In the case of our samples, we tested both chemical and electrochemical methods to synthesize the porous Si. As explained above, the slime green and slime orange colors are observed in the chemical method and black (dark) substrate is created in the electrochemical methods. The porous Si, produced by each of two methods, has a very small absorption under UV radiations, where we have tested the proposed structure as a detector. Therefore, we believe that the fluorescence property of porous silicon does not cause any problem for testing it under UV light.

It is interesting to note that an ideal porous Si substrate for detecting the radiations with the wavelength of X, is a porous Si with absorption peaks at X-wavelength. But, as the wavelength of UV radiations is much smaller in comparison with the absorption peak of porous Si, it is not possible to produce a porous Si with absorption peaks in UV spectrum, even with controlling different parameters in the production process. Such a shift in the PL of porous Si is usually done by controlling the etching parameters, when the porous Si is used as the solar cell or IR detector.

Graphene quantum dots were synthesized using the easy and low-cost bottom-up pyrolysis method of citric acid as the raw material, a previously reported green procedure [31,37]. In brief, 2 g of citric acid was poured into a beaker. The beaker was then placed in an oven at 200 °C. After about 15 min, citric acid was converted from solid to liquid, changing color to pale yellow. As heating continued, the formation of graphene quantum dots was determined by the color change of the solution to orange. After reaching the ambient temperature, the solution was neutralized by adding 1 M NaOH solution under vigorous stirring to a pH of 7, and an aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained. The characteristics of graphene quantum dots can be adjusted by controlling the pyrolysis conditions [38]. The Tauc plot was used to calculate the optical energy gap of the graphene quantum dots solution. Fig. 1 .a briefly depicts the synthesis procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQDs/Porous Si heterojunction photodetector
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared solution of graphene quantum dots was drop-coated onto the Porous Si structure's surface to fabricate the detector. This process was repeated three times for better layering to achieve a more uniform surface distribution, and the structure was dried at ambient temperature. A thin layer of aluminum was deposited on the backside of the wafer to obtain an ohmic connection with silicon, and a top contact with the graphene quantum dots was made using silver paste. A device without GQDs coating was also made for comparison. A schematic diagram of the device is depicted in Fig. 1.b.

A typical TEM image was used to investigate the structural characteristics of GQDs (Fig. 2 ). According to the image, the size distribution of GQDs ranged from 4 to 21 nm. The histogram in Fig. 2.b proved that the average particle diameter is 11.14 nm. The result of UV-VIS spectroscopy and investigation of the behavior of GQDs during light excitation with wavelengths ranging from 200 to 800 nm is given in Fig. 2.c, exhibiting two absorption peaks in the UV region located at 256 nm and 352 nm. From the wavelength of 400 nm onwards, the drop in the graph indicates that the solution has little absorption in the visible region and is somehow transparent. The first peak at 256 nm indicates π→
π *
electron transition in CC bond. Based on the Citric acid (raw material) and carbonization during the reaction process, this absorption wavelength indicates the presence of chromophore groups in the structure, and also a relatively large amount of delocalization of π bonds, which are responsible for electrical conductivity. The second absorption peak at 352 nm is related to n→
π *
transition, which is related to oxygenated functional group [38]. These peaks indicate the sensitivity of GQDs in the UV region and confirm their selection in the UV photodetector structure. The GQDs solution is pale yellow in ambient light (visible), as shown in the inset of Fig. 2.c left, while emitting blue light under UV light excitation (inset of Fig. 2.c right). This emission source is reported due to particle size, various functional groups, and sp2 domain in the sp3 matrix [6,31]. Reducing the size and quantum confinement effect creates an energy gap for GQDs. Using the UV–Vis spectrum, the optical band gap in GQDs was calculated to be 2.94 eV by the Tauc plot approach [39], as illustrated in Fig. 2.d. The π→
π *
electron transition as a result of sp2 electron excitation in carbon atoms and the excitation which resulted in n→
π *
transition are the reason for other probable energy gaps in the Tauc plot. FTIR spectroscopy was performed to determine the functional groups on the GQDs surfaces. The resulting spectrum indicates the presence of carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups that contributed to GQDs adsorption. The absorption band at 3251 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of O–H. The C–H stretching vibrations is located at 3182 cm−1, and a vibrational absorption peak at 11630 cm−1 represent the CO in the carboxyl group. The optical properties of GQDs synthesized with the same approach as we have done in this paper are studied through the Photoluminescent (PL) spectroscopy in Ref. [38]. The PL spectrum is shown in Fig. 2.f. The Photo Luminescence spectra of most carbon nanoparticles are dependent on excitation wavelength. In other words, the PL peaks shift to longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength is a bathochromic shift. But, on the other hand, it is reported in previous studies that GQDs show an excitation-independent PL feature [38,40–43]. This behavior is exactly what we have shown in Fig. 2F inside the manuscript. As demonstrated in this figure, upon excitation with various wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm, the position of the peaks remained constant (∼460 nm), indicating that the fluorescence origin is significantly independent of the excitation wavelength. In other words, when the excitation wavelength changes from 300 to 400 nm, the PL spectra are almost invariable and show a strong peak at∼ 460 nm. This behavior also indicates that the obtained GQDs have uniform size and emissive sites [40]. It is interesting to note that a Stokes shift (∼100 nm) can be seen in the absorption peak in UV–Vis spectrum (∼360 nm) in respect to PL maximum emission at ∼460 nm.

After Ag particle deposition on Si substrate and etching procedure, the morphology of the silicon surface was studied using a FESEM image. Fig. 3 .a depicts silver particles that are somewhat spherical in shape, have an average diameter of approximately 68 nm, and densely cover the entire surface of the silicon. These particles serve as the local cathode during the production of Porous Si in MACE. According to the top view image of the wafer in Fig. 3.b, porous Si was produced evenly on the entire surface of the wafer after the etching procedure. Fig. 3c and 3d, which depict a magnified top view and cross-section view of a wafer, respectively, reveal a wire-like columnar structure consisting of linked holes perpendicular to the surface. The diameter range of Porous Si is 75–85 nm, while their average length is about 29.2 μm. The structure of Porous Si is slightly curved and agglomerated at the tip of the Porous Si, possibly owing to their long length, the forces due to dangling bonds, and the electrostatic charges on the surface that lead to mutual absorption [17]. Fig. 3.e shows the GQDs-coated Porous Si SEM image that is zoomed in Fig. 3.f.

The electrical characteristics of the devices were investigated in the dark and under UV light illumination with a wavelength of 395 nm (near UV or weak UV) and a light intensity of 1400 W/cm2 before and after drop-coating GQDs on the surface of Porous Si. On a linear and semilogarithmic scale, Fig. 4 a and 4b depict the devices' current-voltage characteristic curve (I–V) in the dark and under illumination in the linear and semilogarithmic views, respectively. The device demonstrates a non-linear behavior in both dark and illumination conditions, as can be observed in Fig. 4 (similar to a Schottky diode). Due to the large Schottky barrier and contact potential in GQDs/Si heterojunction, the dark current of the device is decreased in comparison with the bare porous Si samples in both forward and reverse modes. Under the illumination of UV light, the current increases in both bare and GQDs coated porous Si junctions. This increase is considerably larger in GQDs coated samples in comparison with the bare Si devices. The larger photocurrent in GQDs coated junctions is due to the high UV absorption and multiple excitations in GQDs. The detection mechanism will be explained in more detail in the following “Discussion” part.

Some junction parameters, e.g., the ideality factor, saturation current, and barrier height, can be determined using the semilogarithmic diagram of the dark current-voltage in the forward bias region and the thermionic term used for the diodes. The following is the relationship between current and voltage in the Schottky junctions [44]:

According to the abovementioned equations, the saturation current, potential barrier, and the ideality factor for the proposed structure are calculated 28 nA, 0.83eV, and 8.3, respectively. The Richardson constant and junction area are considered to be B = 120 A/cm2K2 for n-type silicon and A = 0.7 cm2. Having inhomogeneity in the contacts, defects in the structures, and the presence of high-density trap levels at the junction interface due to silicon nanostructures, can result in a large value of ideality factor [18,20,33]. The value of the Schottky potential barrier, 0.83eV, is in good accordance with the theoretical obtained value (0.88eV), which is more explained in the next “Discussion” part. The small difference between the values of the Schottky potential barriers obtained through simulation and measurement can be due to the porosity of the silicon surface. The bandgap of porous silicon is slightly larger than that of bulk silicon, and this is what we have neglected in our theoretical calculations.

By applying ultraviolet light (λ = 395 nm, 1400 μW/cm2) to the structures, an increase in current is observed in both structures. The structure without GQDs shows a light current of 49.78 μA at a sample voltage of −10V, while the presence of GQDs in the structure shows a significant increase in current up to 0.731 mA. Light illumination results in a five-time improvement in light current in the Porous Si structure, while this improvement is 50 times in the GQDs/Porous Si structure at a sample voltage of −10V. In the GQDs/Porous Si structure, the current is voltage-dependent in both forward and reverse biases, and as the voltage increases, the current changes. The voltage dependency of the current in reverse bias mode is due to the image force lowering effect of the Schottky junctions. The resistance of the two structures is calculated in the dark and under radiation and at −10V bias (Table 1 ). Evidently, the device's resistance is increased by adding graphene quantum dots to the structure. Under illumination, the resistance decreases with an increase in the light current.

The light-to-dark current ratio is defined as the on/off ratio, and its value for the two structures under reverse bias is illustrated in Fig. 4.c. This parameter in the porous Si structure does not greatly depend on voltage. The highest value for the On/Off ratio in the GQDs/Porous Si structure is 50.12 at −10 V bias and 5.25 for the bare Porous Si. We believe that improving the optical absorption and effective separation of carriers due to the presence of GQDs in the GQDs/porous Si structure leads to this ten-fold increase. Photo-response is examined by photocurrent, i.e., the difference between the illumination current and dark current (I ph = I light-I dark); it depends on the light power density and increases from 7.2 μA to 40.3 μA in bare Porous Si and from 0.28 mA to 0.71 mA in GQDs/Porous Si device with raising power from 21 μW/cm2 to 1400 μW/cm2 at −10V bias voltage. Based on this parameter, the sensitivity (S) of the device is calculated as follows [11,47]:

The sensitivity diagram is displayed in terms of different illumination powers from 21 μW/cm2 to 1400 μW/cm2 at −10 V bias for both structures in Fig. 4.d. Moreover, Fig. 4.e depicts the light current variation due to the illumination of these different UV light powers into the two structures. The GQDs/Porous Si structure is more sensitive compared to the bare Porous Si. Increasing the light power density leads to a growth in the number of photo-generated charge carriers. The diagrams show the rise in light current with increasing the light power density and the applied voltage, which results in an increasing trend of photocurrent. As a result, sensitivity rises almost linearly. The maximum sensitivity values obtained for GQDs/Porous Si and Porous Si structures are 4912.19 and 425.13, respectively, which experience an 11.5-time rise at 1400 μW/cm2 power density.

The parameters described above were calculated for bias voltages of −3 V, −5 V, and −10 V and Area = 0.7 cm2 in Porous Si and GQDs/Porous Si heterojunction, and they are illustrated in terms of applied light power density in Fig. 5 . All these parameters increase with raising the voltage. These changes rise with increasing bias voltage from 3 to 10 V at a specific constant light power density. Maximum values are obtained at a lower power density (21 μW/cm2). The highest responsivity value of 19.41 A/W was obtained in the GQDs/Porous Si structure (Fig. 5 b) under 395 nm light, while for the Porous Si structure (Fig. 5 a), this value was relatively low (approximately 0.5 A/W). The optoelectrical characteristics of the GQDs/Porous Si heterostructure are strengthened due to the combination of excellent structure features of Porous Si with outstanding light absorption characteristics and superior electrical conductivity of GQDs. The recombination rate in the structure grows as the power density increases, the number of photo-generated charge carriers rises, and the trap levels and defects gradually fill up, thereby diminishing responsivity by raising the light power density. The detectivity of the structure also increases after graphene quantum dot drop-coating from 23.10 × 1010 Jones to 7.5 × 1012 Jones. The presence of Porous Si in the structure of GQDs/Porous Si considerably improves the effective interfaces between the two substances. Photo-generated charge carriers travel shorter distances in the structure, and their collection and transmission by GQDs are faster and more efficient, which significantly increases the EQE up to 6093%. Table 2 provides data on this work and some previous studies. As can be seen, the detection parameters are measured at different bias points. When the detector works on the Schottky effect, the photo-generated current is larger at a larger reverse bias voltage due to the image force lowering [45,46]. On the other hand, at a larger voltage bias point, the thermal noise also increases, and there is usually a tradeoff between larger noise and larger responsivity for choosing the bias point [45,46]. As can be deduced from Table 2, the UV detectors based on nanostructures [51–64], and wide bandgap materials [9,55] have larger responsivity in comparison with other structures. This is while most wide-bandgap materials have an expensive production process and nanomaterials have a complex production process. The proposed GQDs/Porous Si Schottky junction has a low-cost chemical production process, while its photo response to UV radiations is large and acceptable among other Si-based detectors. The theoretical justification is explained in the next section in detail.

The initial stage in the working process of a photodetector is to irradiate light into the structure and form photo-generated electron-hole pairs. The resulting pairs, after some time, known as recombination time, joint and return to the first state. Before they can be recombined, these photo-generated charge carriers must be separated. In the last stage, the electrodes collect the split charge carriers and send them to an external circuit to produce the current. Because electrons and holes have different electric charges, it is possible to separate the produced pairs by creating an electric field and generating a current. Inherently produced fields in various structures, such as the electric field in the depletion region, can be employed to accomplish this separation. Current is made in the external circuit with the help of this electric field and the separation of the electron-hole pairs in the depletion region. Such an electric field results in a rectifying behavior in current-voltage characteristics. Looking back to Fig. 4a and 4.b, which show the I–V curves for bare porous Si sample and GQDs/Porous Si heterojunction. As can be seen from I–V curves, the current is rectified in both samples, which is a result of a built-in electric field rises from the potential barrier. One interesting point that can be understood from these figures is that the dark current is decreased in the samples with GQDs. This issue shows that there is probably a bigger potential barrier in front of the carrier flow in GQDs/porous Si samples in comparison with bare porous Si samples. To clear this question, the energy band diagram for the bare porous Si sample and GQDs/porous Si heterojunction are shown in Fig. 6 a and 6b. The work function of Si is calculated 4.3eV, considering (1-10) Ω.cm, n-type Si wafer. In the bare porous Si samples, two metal electrodes, Ag and Al, are contacted to the porous Si as anode and cathode terminals, respectively. As Ag has a larger work function than Si in our samples, therefore, the electrons move from Si to Ag and form a Schottky barrier at the interface (see Fig. 6a). The height of this potential barrier is about 0.4eV. The creation of this Schottky barrier is the main reason for the rectifying behavior of the current-voltage curve, as previously shown in Fig. 4a and 4.b. The built-in electric field in the depletion region of this Schottky junction is what has separated the photo-generated carriers in the bare porous Si samples and resulted in responsivity of 0.49A/W.

When GQDs are deposited on the porous Si substrate, a different mechanism is created to generate the photocurrent, in which GQD has the main role. When GQD with the large bandgap (2–4)eV [56,57] is deposited on a porous Si substrate, a heterojunction is created at the interface. As GQD is not doped with alkali metals [58], it is expected that its work function is around 5.2 eV [56–58]. Due to Fig. 6.b, the electrons move from Si to GQDs just after the GQDs deposition, and a large potential barrier is created at the interface after this movement. The height of this potential barrier is calculated 0.88eV, which is in good accordance with the value of 0.83eV obtained from the measured curves. To make sure that it is GQD that causes the optical response in our proposed structure, we look back to Fig. 4a, 4b, and 4.e, which demonstrate the (I–V) curves under UV illuminations. When a metal or metal-like material creates a Schottky junction with a semiconductor and is irradiated with light, two factors may cause the photocurrent [45,46,59,60]. First, the photo-generated carriers inside the semiconductor, and second the internal photoemission of the carriers inside the metal or metal-like material. What determines which factor is dominant in the optical response is the shape of the current-voltage curve. When the (I–V) curve does not enter the fourth quarter under the illumination; in other words, the device does not produce power, it shows that the internal photoemission of the carriers has increased the carrier energies and generated the photo response [45,46,59,60]. This is exactly what happened in GQDs/porous Si heterojunction under UV illuminations. The UV-generated carriers are excited in GQDs through internal photoemission. Those carriers whose momentums are perpendicular to the junction interface enter the semiconductor (Si) and increase the current [61]. It is worth noting that this explanation does not mean that silicon does not absorb UV radiations or that UV rays do not produce carriers in silicon in our proposed GQDs/porous Si heterojunction. It means that the photocurrent is dominated by the carrier generation in GQDs rather than Si. Especially in the proposed structure, the Si surface is covered by GQDs, and the UV radiations are absorbed by GQDs before reaching the silicon.

On the other hand, GQD has a large UV absorption which increases the photo response. The combination of GQDs and Porous Si and the creation of the heterostructure improve light absorption by creating a silicon nanostructure and having a greater surface area; moreover, due to the high absorption of GQDs in the ultraviolet region and their excellent electrical conductivity, the number of charge carriers produced by light absorption is higher, and their transmission is faster in the GQDs/Porous Si than the Porous Si structure. Since in the porous structure, the effective photo detection area is considerably increased and the depletion region of the junction is extended over a larger area in comparison with the junction with planar semiconductors, the photo generated carriers have more chances to be separated by the electric field, thus the recombination of the carriers is decreased, and the adsorption efficiency is improved. The outcome of this absorption improvement is evident in the enhanced optical response of the structure. Therefore, the GQDs in the structure play two roles: 1) production and conduction of charge carriers and 2) setting up a barrier against electrons and preventing recombination in the structure. It should be noted that multiple excitations in GQDs lead to a high external efficiency which is a well-known effect in GQDs photodetectors [6,7,9,10,13,29,32,33]. The use of this semiconductor material and the stated mechanism helps improve the photodetector properties and provide an appropriate response.

The speed of a UV detector depends on the detection mechanism and the material used in its structure. From detection mechanism point of view, the detectors are mainly categorized as vacuum UV detectors and solid-state UV detectors [62,63]. Due to the detection mechanism of solid-state detectors which is based on the pair generation under UV radiation, the speed of this type of UV detectors are faster than the vacuum ones. The solid-state UV detectors are categorized to photoconductors, Schottky junctions, p-n and p-i-n junctions and MSM(Metal-Semiconductor-Metal) junctions, from carrier-collection mechanism point of view. Among the solid-state UV detectors, the photoconductors have low speed and large dark current [62,63]. The other types (Schottky, p-i-n and MSM) are considered as fast or high-speed UV detectors. Comparing the speed of these detectors depends on the material and specific nanostructures used in their design. The materials that are commonly used in the structure of the solid-state UV detectors are Si (due to its abundancy and low cost), metallic oxides (e.g., ZnO, TiO2, SnO2, VO2 and Ga2O3), iii-v semiconductors (e.g., GaN and AlN) and SiC, due to their wide-energy bandgap [62]. It has been shown that using the nanostructures like Nano Wires (NWs) and Quantum Dots (QDs) increase the speed in addition to the efficiency of the UV detectors. The main reason for this improvement is that the reduced dimensionality of nanostructures shortens the transient time and increase the speed [62]. For example, ZnO NWs on Graphene UV detector has the response and recovery time of 9.5s and 38s [64]. The TiO2 NWs UV detectors has the response time of 0.4s [67]. Kinked SnO2 NWs UV detector has a shorter response time than straight-forward SnO2 UV detector [52]. The UV detector based on β-Ga2O3 NWs are one of the fast detectors with decay time of less than 10μs [68]. It has been shown in Ref. [24] that porosity of Si has decreased the response and decay times to <1 ms in comparison with the UV detector based on bulk Si.

On the other hand, using of the hybrid nanostructures has increased the detector speed. For example, the n-ZnO/n-GaN UV photodetectors decorated with GQDs by Liu et al. shows a fast response rate and current increase dramatically from off state after UV illumination to on state. In the absence of GQDs, the rise time is 260 ms and decay time is 250 ms, while at presence of GQDs in the structure these numbers significantly decrease to 100 ms and 120 ms, respectively [69]. After deposition of GQDs on MoS2, in the hybrid structure reported by Min, Misook et al. they realized that the structure shows faster rise and decay time compared with bare MoS2 [29]. After sensitizing TiO2 with GQDs, the self-powered GQDs sensitized TiO2 photodetector was investigated under UV light, and it indicates great switching behavior, with rise time of 0.12s and decay time of 0.03s [10]. Our presented GQDs/Porous Si Schottky UV detector benefits from two effects that are previously reported to increase the speed of the UV detectors. Those effects are porosity of the semiconductor and the utility of GQDs. Therefore, we expect the response and decay times of our proposed UV detector to be short, resulting in a high-speed detector. It is worth to note that the experiments are underway to study the dynamic photo response of GQDs/Porous Si heterojunction under modulated UV radiations.

In addition to UV radiations, we have tested our proposed GQDs/porous Si Schottky detector under IR (850 nm and 940 nm), white and red illuminations. The current-voltage curve is shown in Fig. 7 .a. The reverse mode is zoomed in Fig. 7.b. To investigate the photodetector behavior under different light power, we measured the photo-generated current under different light power and the device was illuminated with 850 nm and 940 nm light under two different light powers, as shown in Fig. 8 a. The photocurrent (I light –I dark) is also shown in Fig. 8.b. As can be seen, by increasing the light power density, the photocurrent is increased at all bias points as expected. The measured dark current is 9.7 μA under 10 V reverse bias, and the maximum photocurrent under 850 nm illumination (with power density of 27.5 w/m2) is measured to be 0.35 mA and it is 0.37 mA under 940 nm (81.4 w/m2) light irradiation.

The results show that as the light power is increased, the number of photons is also increased. Therefore, it is expected to observe a growth in the photo-generated current. This is exactly what we have shown in Fig. 8.a. Additionally, it is observed in Fig. 8b, 8c and 8d that the responsivity, detectivity and external efficiency are also dependent to the power of the source, and decrease by increasing the power of the light source. We think this effect is probably caused by two reasons. First, the recombination rate in the structure grows as the power density increases, the number of photo-generated charge carriers rises, and the trap levels and defects gradually fill up, thereby diminishing responsivity by raising the light power density [7–29]. The second is the saturation of the photo-generated current by raising the light power. In other words, the current cannot be increased more or equal to the increment of the light power. Therefore, the responsivity (Photocurrent/Light power) decreases by increasing the light power. Responsivity decrement due to the power raising is a well-known effect and is usually observed during the detector testing process. Also, it is shown in Fig. 8b, 8c and 8.d that by increasing the reversed bias voltage, the responsivity, detectivity and efficiency are increased. It is due to the well-known image force lowering effect that decrease the potential barrier in front of the carriers in reversed bias mode in Schottky junctions.

Herein, a GQDs/Porous Si heterostructure has been fabricated and investigated as an ultraviolet light detector. The findings revealed that this device has a good response under near-UV radiation with a wavelength of 395 nm. Porous Si was produced using the cost-effective, simple MACE approach, and GQDs were fabricated using citric acid via the bottom-up pyrolysis method and drop-coated on the substrate. The structure was assessed in the dark and under UV radiation. In the presence of GQDs in the structure, under a −10 V bias, the values of R, D, and EQE were 19.41 A/W, 7.5 × 1012 Jones, 6093%, respectively; in bare Porous Si, however, these values were 0.49 A/W, 23.7 × 1010 Jones, and 95%, respectively. It can thus be claimed that the presence of GQDs in the structure enhances the optical detection properties.

Fatemeh Kalantari: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Mina Amirmazlaghani: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Saeed Olyaee: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
